speaker: interviewer
text: Date, July 13, 1995. Survivor, Elizabeth Deutsch, maiden name Egri. Interviewer, Francene Rotenberg. City, Fairfield, state, Connecticut, country, United States of America, language, English. My name is Francine Rotenberg. Today's date is July 13, 1995. I'm interviewing Elizabeth Deutsch in Fairfield, Connecticut, United States of America. The language is English. Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Elizabeth Egri Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: E-G-R-I, my maiden name, and my married name is D-E-U-T-S-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew names, they used to call me Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Esther. And when is your birthdate?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's August 23, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us the city and the country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came from-- I was born in Bodrogkeresztur in Hungary, and that was in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's B-O-D-R-O-G-K-E-R-E-S-Z-T-U-R. It's a long name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me what you were like as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very interesting. As a young child growing up in a village, I lived with my parents and three brothers-- older brothers-- and one older sister. And I also had my grandparents, and aunts, and uncles, and cousins. And we were a big family and a very close knit family. In our little village, everybody was very close to each other. It was really like a big family. Population was about 3,500 people. And among the people, we were about 600 Jewish people. We had two synagogues. We had lots of stores, small stores. As a matter of fact, my grandparents had one of them, a grocery store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these your mother's parents or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's parents, yes. Because we were living in Hungary, and my father was living, actually, in Czechoslovakia. In home in Europe, they used to have [NON-ENGLISH]. And I don't know exactly the story how they met, but my father wind up in Hungary and married my mother. And so it was a quiet, interesting place to live in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a child growing up, it was-- you know, with older-- mostly the Jewish people, they lived on main street. But houses were scattered around the valleys and the hills. So also we had Bodrog, and people used to come and bathe there in the summertime, like a summer place, from other town around. And we had a post office. We had a townhouse. We had a police station. We didn't have inside plumbing. We didn't have no telephone, only one in the post office, and perhaps the police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had schools, yes. And matter of fact, they had to build onto it because the population, you know, were getting bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go-- did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school. And afterwards, I went to cheder in the afternoon. So and I also had a [NON-ENGLISH] who came to the house and teach me Hebrew. And so that was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of subjects did you learn in school? Did you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, everything, just like you would learn here. And we were walking to school every day. There was no transportation with the car. And it didn't matter if it was raining or snowing. That didn't stop the school not being open. And so the life in Bodrogkeresztur was very smoothly going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you an orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very orthodox family. My father was a very pious Jew. Matter of fact, he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You must probably heard about that. They had [NON-ENGLISH] and others. So he was very, very religious man. And we, of course, everybody was kosher there. And what was so interesting is we had a very, very, very big garden. And we growed our own vegetables, and it was so convenient just to go down to garden to pick up one fruit or one vegetable or whatever you needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then once a week we had in our village like a-- oh gee, what do you call it? People came there selling stuff. Market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Market. Yes, market. So other people used to come in town and shop around there. So we had our own chickens. We had our on cows and horses. And I remember I used to love to drink the nice fresh milk as the milk-- cow was milked. And the chicken when we needed one for Shabbos, we just catched one and took it to the shochet and, you know, she-- he prepared it and we had our meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a businessman together with my uncle. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were going to market to buy things, livestock or things like that, and then they sell. Before that, we used to have a grocery store. My sister was telling me this because I don't remember. That was before, you know, earlier years. And so we had a very, very famous rabbi who lived in our village. His name was Steiner Shayele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember him because he died before I was born. But our village was so famous that when you mentioned Bodrogkeresztur, everybody know where it was because people were coming there from all over at the world, actually. He was so educated, and he could help the poor and give advice for the sick. And after he died, they buried him-- even today, there is a house there in the cemetery where quite a few rabbis are buried over there. And they go to their graves. And so our little town was bustling all around all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any friends who weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yeah. Well, my neighbor, one of them actually, they were non-Jews. We were friendly in the school. We were getting along fine. And that was really, you know, no problem. And on Shabbos, when we welcomed the Shabbos Friday evening, it was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? Tell us what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, my mother used to put on the nicest dress, you know, she had and a white apron, and of course she was always wearing a kerchief-- either a kerchief or a sheitel. And she would light the 11 candles because we were 11 children originally. I had two set of twin sisters, but they died, I understand, as soon as they were born. They were the very first one. And my father, before he would go to shul, he would call me-- I was the youngest. The other one were older, or some of my brother weren't home. So he would put his hand on my head. He would bless me every Shabbos. You know, Friday evening, he would go to shul. And that stays with me. I remember that so well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the boys go with him to shul too, and you stayed home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. Yeah, whoever was home, they went. And actually, over there, the ladies really didn't go so much on Shabbos to the synagogue. Mostly on the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do then on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Shabbos, well, it was a very quiet relaxing day after a very busy week. Because over there a housewife was very busy. We didn't have no Frigidaire, you know? We had to-- my mother had to cook every single day, just for the day, but there was no place to put the leftover. And we did have a cellar where we kept, you know, like the potatoes what we dug up in the fall and put it away. That was underground, not under the house. The houses were built from very, very heavy stones. And so we really didn't need air condition because it was-- the house was pretty cold. In our climate where I was living, it wasn't bad that time. May be changed since 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so like we made our own butter. [NON-ENGLISH]-- you want to know how I made-- how we made it? How you made it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big container where they put the milk. And then on the cellar steps, it was standing I don't know for how many days, until this heavy-- they called it butter fat or whatever, it come up the top of the milk. And then they used to take it off. And we had a container where we put that in, and we had to do this and that for hours till butter came out, but it was so delicious with the nice fresh bread. I forgot to tell you something. Before Shabbos, my mother was already busy. Thursday afternoon she started to bake challah and cake. And we had an oven in the yard. And the bottom was from brick. And we had to put wood in it and burn it quite a long time before it really got good and warm so we could bake in there. And that's where we baked the bread and mostly everything. But we had a baker there too. Sometime if you didn't want to bother with it, you could take it there or you could go to the bakery and buy some bread. But my mother, she rest in peace, she liked to do those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitic episodes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not, not when I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first get an inkling that something wasn't right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could feel in 1940 when the war started to broke out in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old then about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my goodness, how old was I then? Maybe 13, 14, or 12, I don't know. But as the years were going by, it was getting worse and worse. So in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening? How was it getting worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took the men away for labor camp. Like my brother was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest, very oldest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Jeno. And they were taking him away, and we didn't know where he was. We used to get cards, post cards--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942 or '43. I can't recall it exactly. Then they took my second brother away. They took my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adolf. And then they took my father away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they, when you say they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- just the-- they were called in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it go? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were called in. They just told them they have to go into this unit. We didn't know where they were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told them that? Was it the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian. The Hungarian, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it, a policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian police, yeah, the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would come to your door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Door, yes. And, and told that they have to go, but we never know where they were. When we used to get a card, it was only number on it. And that was their address. If I would write to them, I would put their name on it and the number. They had like a secret code or whatever. And so they took my uncle away, who was my grandfather's son, because my sister took care of my grandparents. And that was her job to do. And so then we know already something it wasn't right. Then later on, the rationing started to come. The food, they would just give you-- we had to stay in line to pick up flours, sugar, and bread. And they were just giving so much to a family to live on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was getting to be very, you know, difficult. And later on already, end of 1943, we had to darken our windows so no light was going out from the house because airplanes used to go over, and they didn't want-- you know, it was just pitch dark. How did you know to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you told? Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we really didn't have radio over there, but when it was any special news coming, a man would come from the town house, and he had drum. And he would play his drum, and then we know that we have to go out to the street and listen what he has to read. And he's the one who was reading out the news and the information, what we got and what we have to do. That's what we get. Some people would have radio. Probably was going with batteries. And we would, like, congregate there, you know, and listen, but very seldom do that. We had a newspaper too. We had to pick up our mail. There was no delivery. And things was very, very different like over here. Example, here it's so easy to go out to the kitchen and put the faucet on and get the water. Over there in home, we had a well. Matter of fact, we had two. One was the good water, and the other one we used for the animals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so living over there, it wasn't so convenience as over here, but that's how we know, you know? We didn't know any better, and we really enjoyed our life the way it was. After your brothers were taken to labor camps, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's only my mother, my sister and after a while, my father was sent back home because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your father was taken too?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was taken too. Matter of fact, that's where they shaved his beautiful beard. He was such a handsome man before they took him away. He had blue eyes and tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benny Egri. And he-- every time, you know, he was walking, people just loved him. He had the smiling eyes. He would say-- he would go by anyone not to say a word, and they always used to say, here comes the smiling man. And when they took him to the labor camp, and they shaved his beard off--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that must have been in 1942 or '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live then with all your brothers away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was only my mother and my sister and grandfather was home. So we moved in with grandfather. Grandmother passed away between must have been maybe 1942 or '41. And so we lived in the grandfather's house with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who lived there in your grandfather's house now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, father, and my sister. And, well, my two brothers were away. And the youngest of the three, he was away too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he went to learn a trade to be a baker. And after he finished, then he was working in the next town, so he wasn't home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarcal. It wasn't that far. We used to walk a lot home. And so we really-- only the ladies were home, except my uncle who was together with my father later on. He was home with his family, who was my mother's sister, my aunt. And they had five children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so we were kind of busy. I always had friends because before that, we shared a house. Like on one side they were living, and the other side we were living. So we had kids all around all the time. And I also had other friends who I had, but after school I had to come home first and do my chore, what I supposed to do. After that, I could only go out and play and do whatever was necessary. And then it was a certain time I had to be home, but certain things had to be done. Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you, we had a lot of chickens. My job was to let them go into their little house. And then I was helping my mother set the table for the dinner. And whatever had to be done. I really didn't mind. I was very willing to, to learn and to help her and do things around the house. I remember when-- excuse me. When-- let me just have a little water. [DRINKS] When grandmother was alive, she rest in peace, she had such a patient. And she taught me a lot. Home, we did everything ourself-- made the noodles and bake, as I told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the very, very first time, I couldn't reach the table. I had to stand up on a little stool. And she made me, from 10 eggs, she made homemade noodles. It came out pretty good. Let me tell you something, you know? I said, so Grandma, what am I going to do? I don't know. Just do what I tell you to do. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I learned a lot from her. I learned a lot from my mother. And my upbringing was very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you shared the house, do you mean when you lived just with your parents, or when you moved into your grandparents' house? Or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. When my parents were home, we shared the house. Like it was a, you know, long house. So one half we lived, and the other half, the uncle lived and his five and the family. And we had stables and we had-- over there, we had very, very big gardens. The same with grandfather's house too. He had a big house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about when you already moved to your grandfather's house. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, over there it was already then already was getting near the war, coming closer and closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- oh, yes. I was going to school. I was a young girl. 1944, I was about 15 years old. And it was very scary because I remember one time when my father went up to Budapest because he wasn't a Hungarian citizen. And they were going to take us away if he couldn't get any paper to tell where he came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: So while he was away, the Hungarian police came to our house and they took us away-- my mother, my sister and myself to the jail up to Budapest [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] because we didn't have no paper. But you see, I was born in really in Hungary. I was a Hungarian citizen, and so was my mother and sister. And so finally, my father was able to get some papers and show it to them, to the police. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. And they let us come out. We just slept there overnight. And the next day, we were coming home. So that was a very something to remember, you know? And so life over there after that was getting very, very difficult. I remember a Polish young man escaped from Poland. And he was telling us stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Jewish or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Jewish. He was Jewish. And he came to Mad. That was another town not too far from us. And he was in the family. It was my uncle's sister's place where he stopped. And he was telling stories, unbelievable stories what they were doing with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killing people. They had to dig their hole and, you know, make him stand there, and they were shooting them. And they would just fall in there. And we couldn't believe. We just didn't believe it. We thought he was, you know, crazy. He was making up stories. That can't happen, such a thing. And he stayed with the family. He stayed with the family for the time, till March that year in 1944. I remember it was March 19 when the German SS man came into Hungary.
